prompt:
A generic medical-product instruction illustration in simple black line-art on a pure white background, in the universal patient-information-leaflet style. Subject: a hand holding the CAPPED blood-filled vial vertically between THUMB AND INDEX FINGER ONLY (a two-point pinch grip — thumb on the BOTTOM, index finger on the TOP), with curved arrows around it indicating an INVERSION / FLIPPING motion. A '10X' text label in the upper-left corner indicates this should be repeated 10 times.

HAND POSE — TWO-POINT PINCH (CRITICAL):
- The hand has exactly FIVE FINGERS total: ONE THUMB + INDEX + MIDDLE + RING + PINKY. Do NOT draw extra fingers, fingertips, or duplicated digits. Count carefully: 1 thumb, 4 fingers, 5 total.
- The vial is held VERTICALLY between just the THUMB and the INDEX FINGER, like pinching a small pencil between its two ends.
- THUMB at the BOTTOM: the thumb is extended out from the hand, its pad pressing against the BOTTOM (closed glass tip) of the vial from below, supporting the vial vertically.
- INDEX FINGER at the TOP: the index finger is extended out from the hand, its pad pressing against the TOP of the cap (on top of the vial's ribbed cap), pressing down lightly.
- The thumb and index finger are roughly OPPOSITE each other along the vial's vertical axis — vial sandwiched between them along its length.
- The remaining fingers (middle, ring, pinky) are CURLED INWARD into a loose fist behind the vial — they DO NOT touch the vial. Only the thumb (bottom) and index (top) are in contact with the vial.
- The hand is positioned with the palm roughly facing the viewer, fingers projecting upward. Wrist trails off naturally at the bottom of the frame.
- This pose makes the inversion motion obvious — by rotating the wrist 180°, the user can flip the vial upside-down and back, with the thumb and index swapping their positions.

VIAL (locked spec, CAPPED, blood-filled):
- Short stubby clear glass tube held vertically with the cap at the top.
- A small RIBBED CAP with vertical knurling/grip lines on its outer side wall sits on top of the vial. Flat solid top on the cap. The stepped rim that was on the open vial is now HIDDEN beneath the cap.
- PARTIALLY FILLED with SOLID RED BLOOD up to ~55% of the vial's height from the bottom. Solid red fill, clean flat meniscus on top, empty clear glass above.
- Double-line graduation ring around midpoint of the body.
- Single faint highlight stripe on one side of the glass.
- Plain closed glass end at the bottom (where the thumb touches).

ABSOLUTE SCALE: the hand DOMINATES the canvas. The vial is small (finger-sized) held between just the thumb and index finger. The vial is clearly visible from rim to tip, not enclosed in a fist.

INVERSION MOTION ARROWS:
- TWO LARGE CURVED ARROWS encircling the hand+vial in a counter-rotating loop pattern (one curved arrow on the LEFT side sweeping up and over to the right, a mirrored curved arrow on the RIGHT side sweeping the opposite way) — together they form a complete inversion loop indicating the vial is being flipped end-over-end. Both arrows have clear arrowheads at their tips. Slim line weight matching the rest of the illustration.

'10X' COUNT LABEL: in the UPPER-LEFT corner of the canvas, draw a clean bold '10X' text label (the number 10 followed by capital X). Simple sans-serif lettering, no box, just the characters.

Style: uniform medium-weight black ink lines on clean white. ONE RED allowed: the SOLID RED FILL of the blood inside the vial. Everything else (hand, vial outline, cap, arrows, '10X' text) is pure black on white. Minimal short parallel hatching only for soft shadow / volume on the hand knuckles. No frame, no border, no caption, no other labels, no logos. The inversion arrows and '10X' label are required instructional exceptions. Subject centered in a 1:1 square composition with generous clean white margins. Drawn-by-hand line work, generic patient information leaflet illustration style.
